costs: Setup and ongoing operational cost of a national registry and evaluation infrastructure
Administrative reporting burden on tankerület centers
Time lag before registry accumulates sufficient data to inform decisions meaningfully
content.equity_impact: Positive as an enabling condition: transparent thresholds and outcome tracking make disadvantage-concentration effects (e.g., church-school segregation, Roma enrollment disparities) visible and auditable across all models, supporting evidence-based equity interventions regardless of which structural direction (T1-T4) a given village follows; does not by itself alter distributional outcomes without complementary action.
expected_benefits: Breaks the historical cycle of politically-driven, non-evidence-based policy swings (F-live-implementation_planning-02)
Enables verification of contested cost-savings claims, addressing weaknesses identified in Finnish and US consolidation literature (F-live-finnish_reform-04, F-live-education_finance-02)
Provides Hungary-specific evidence on closure-depopulation linkage rather than relying on non-replicated foreign findings
Improves accountability and consistency without prescribing a single operational model, preserving local flexibility across T1-T4 approaches
content.goal: Establish a national, cross-cutting evidence and transparency framework — publicly visible minimum-viability thresholds, standardized cost accounting, and a closure/outcome registry — that informs but does not mandate any specific local model (status quo, grade-split, network, or consolidation), improving the quality and accountability of decisions made under any of T1-T4.

implementation_steps.action: Design and launch a public closure/retention outcome registry recording enrollment, cost, demographic, and academic indicators for every village school decision
implementation_steps.actor: National statistics office / Ministry of Interior

implementation_steps.action: Publish standardized minimum-viability thresholds and cost-accounting methodology, including transport and adjustment costs, applicable uniformly across tankerület centers
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education (or Interior Ministry education unit)

implementation_steps.action: Conduct mandatory ex-post evaluation of a sample of closure and retention decisions against registry data every 2 years
Standardize training criteria for multigrade, rural, and network teaching roles, tied to registry-documented best practices
Report all closure/retention decisions and rationale to the registry as a condition of decision validity

mechanisms: Historical evidence shows repeated top-down policy swings without institutionalized feedback mechanisms (interwar expansion, Stalinist demolition, 1970s rationalization, 2013 centralization, 2017 re-decentralization), indicating the absence of a standing evidence infrastructure as a root cause of policy instability (F-live-implementation_planning-02, F-live-education_finance-10).

Finnish municipalities cited cost-savings as the primary closure rationale, but few rigorous follow-up studies verified whether savings were realized — indicating a need for mandatory post-decision cost verification (F-live-finnish_reform-04, F-live-education_finance-02).

Contested and mixed cross-national evidence on closure-depopulation linkage (Finland vs. Saxony vs. Sweden) demonstrates the need for a Hungary-specific evidence base rather than reliance on non-replicated foreign findings (F-live-school_network_planning-06, F-live-education_finance-04, F-live-polish_reform-07).

The 2022 finding that only 17% of residents in schoolless municipalities consider the absence a major shortcoming, versus over 81% anticipatory attachment in villages with schools, shows a measurement gap between anticipated and revealed preferences that a registry could track over time (F-live-education_finance-05).

Transport-cost offsets are rarely fully counted in closure proposals, indicating a need for standardized cost-accounting rules covering transportation as well as instructional costs (F-live-education_finance-03).

Legal ambiguity over transport-obligation assignment (municipality vs. district) similarly indicates the need for standardized, transparent procedural rules independent of which operational model is used (F-live-legal_and_governance-05).
risks: Purely informational infrastructure may be circumvented or ignored absent binding decision rules, as seen in the historical pattern of top-down political decisions overriding evidence (F-live-implementation_planning-02, F-live-polish_reform-04)
Given post-2010 curtailment of stakeholder consultation mechanisms, a new registry could be under-resourced or politically deprioritized (F-live-implementation_planning-10)
Data collection itself could be gamed or inconsistently reported by tankerület centers absent independent audit
content.title: National transparency and evidence infrastructure: viability thresholds, cost-visibility, and a closure/outcome registry
